content.text: Approved LLM SDK and Framework Dependency Policy

Enterprise GenAI systems pull in a wide range of third-party SDKs — provider clients, orchestration frameworks, embedding utilities, and prompt-helper libraries. Without a controlled approval process, teams independently adopt conflicting versions, use packages with incompatible licenses, or import libraries that exfiltrate prompts to third-party endpoints. This sub-subtopic defines a tiered approval policy, the CI gates that enforce it, and the RFC process for adding new dependencies.

The goal is not to restrict innovation but to ensure every dependency that touches model I/O has been evaluated for security posture, license compatibility, data-handling guarantees, and architectural fit with the adapter layer.
Why Dependency Governance Matters for GenAI
LLM SDKs are a higher-risk dependency class than typical business libraries for three reasons:
SDK calls carry raw prompts, retrieved context, and model outputs. A compromised or poorly-audited package can silently log or forward this data to third-party endpoints.
Provider APIs and orchestration frameworks evolve quickly. An un-pinned dependency can break prompt construction or tool-calling schemas between minor versions.
Several popular orchestration libraries mix MIT core packages with GPL-licensed plug-ins. Importing the wrong sub-package can impose unwanted obligations on proprietary code.
Rule of thumb: if the library is in the hot path between your prompt template and the model API, it belongs under the approved-dependency policy, not the general dependency policy.
Dependencies are classified into four tiers. Higher tiers require more review steps before a team may add them to a service.
Pin to exact version; upgrade requires re-review
tiktoken, instructor, pydantic-ai, guidance, outlines, litellm
Security + legal review; SBOM entry required
Unmaintained forks, GPL-only packages, packages without vendor support SLA, packages with known data-exfil CVEs
Cannot be added without CISO exception

Tier 1 packages are maintained by the model providers themselves and carry enterprise support agreements. They are pre-approved as a class; only the specific version range needs to be recorded in the project's
The RFC Process for New Dependencies
Any dependency not already on the approved list — regardless of tier eligibility — requires a Dependency Request RFC before it is merged into any production service. The RFC template lives at
and contains the following mandatory sections:
Why is this package needed? What alternatives were evaluated and ruled out?
Does the package make outbound network calls? Does it log input/output? Provide evidence (source review or published privacy policy).
State the SPDX identifier. Confirm it is compatible with the service's own license and any upstream distribution requirements.
Last release date, open CVE count, number of maintainers, vendor SLA (if any).
Does the package couple application code directly to a provider API, or does it fit behind the project's adapter interface? If the former, explain why the adapter pattern cannot be preserved.
Exact version to add initially. How often will the team review and upgrade it?

The RFC is reviewed by the Platform Architecture team (mandatory) and the Security team (mandatory for Tier 3). Legal reviews data-handling sections for any package that touches production user data. Approval is recorded in the RFC as a comment and the package is added to the central allowlist file before any PR that imports it may merge.
A single source of truth for approved dependencies lives at
in the monorepo root (or per-service root in a polyrepo). The schema is:

{
  "approved": [
    {
      "name": "openai",
      "ecosystem": "pypi",
      "tier": 1,
      "version_range": ">=1.30,<2",
      "approved_date": "2024-11-01",
      "rfc": null,
      "notes": "Pre-approved Tier 1"
    },
    {
      "name": "langchain-core",
      "ecosystem": "pypi",
      "tier": 2,
      "version_range": "==0.3.15",
      "approved_date": "2025-02-14",
      "rfc": "dep-rfc-0042",
      "notes": "Architecture review passed; adapter pattern preserved"
    },
    {
      "name": "instructor",
      "ecosystem": "pypi",
      "tier": 3,
      "version_range": "==1.7.0",
      "approved_date": "2025-04-03",
      "rfc": "dep-rfc-0061",
      "notes": "Legal approved; no outbound calls in structured-output mode"
    }
  ]
}
Every entry records the RFC that authorised it. Tier 1 entries have
because they are pre-approved by policy, not by individual RFC.
Three gates in every CI pipeline enforce the allowlist. They run as a dedicated
job that must pass before the build job executes.
A script resolves the project's locked dependency graph (
) and checks every resolved package against
to extract SPDX identifiers and blocks any package with a disallowed license:

# scripts/check_licenses.py
import subprocess, json, sys

BLOCKED_LICENSES = {
    "GPL-2.0-only", "GPL-3.0-only",
    "AGPL-3.0-or-later", "AGPL-3.0-only",
    "SSPL-1.0",
}

result = subprocess.run(
    ["pip-licenses", "--format=json", "--with-system"],
    capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
)
packages = json.loads(result.stdout)

violations = [
    f"BLOCKED LICENSE: {p['Name']} {p['Version']} — {p['License']}"
    for p in packages
    if p["License"] in BLOCKED_LICENSES
]

if violations:
    for v in violations:
        print(v)
    sys.exit(1)

print(f"License check passed: {len(packages)} packages audited")
Tier 2 and Tier 3 packages must be pinned to an exact version in
Integrating the Gates into GitHub Actions

# .github/workflows/dependency-policy.yml
name: Dependency Policy

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'requirements*.txt'
      - 'pyproject.toml'
      - '.approved-dependencies/**'

jobs:
  dependency-policy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt pip-licenses

      - name: Gate 1 — Allowlist
        run: python scripts/check_dependency_allowlist.py

      - name: Gate 2 — Licenses
        run: python scripts/check_licenses.py

      - name: Gate 3 — Version pins
        run: python scripts/check_version_pins.py

      - name: Generate SBOM
        run: |
          pip install cyclonedx-bom
          cyclonedx-py requirements -o sbom.json

      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: sbom
          path: sbom.json
Approved versions are not static. The platform team runs a quarterly dependency review that:
against all Tier 2 and Tier 3 packages to surface new CVEs.
Checks for deprecated API usage (provider SDKs regularly remove legacy endpoints).
Evaluates whether newer versions bring material improvements that justify re-review.
Removes packages from the allowlist when the project that requested them has been decommissioned.
When a CVE of severity HIGH or CRITICAL is disclosed against an approved package:
Security team creates a P1 tracking ticket within 24 hours.
A patch version that resolves the CVE is reviewed under an expedited 48-hour window.
If no patch is available, the package is moved to the Blocked tier and services depending on it receive a 72-hour removal window.
The expedited RFC is filed retrospectively within one sprint.
A dependency policy that engineers cannot discover is a policy that will be violated. Distribute awareness through:
Green/red allowlist status badge on every service repo
Automated comment when a PR adds an unlisted package, linking the RFC template
Newly approved packages, removed packages, CVE advisories
Full allowlist rendered as a searchable table; RFC archive
"Read the Dependency Policy" item in new-engineer onboarding
The PR comment bot is the most effective enforcement mechanism — it intercepts the violation at the exact moment engineers add a new import, when context is freshest and the fix is cheapest.
A Tier 3 package imports a Tier 2 orchestration framework as a transitive dependency. The allowlist check must operate on the fully-resolved lock file, not just direct dependencies. Use
without realising the pin check will block the PR. Document the rule prominently in the requirements file header.
Jupyter notebooks often install packages via
cells that escape CI. Run notebook environments in pre-built Docker images with the allowlist baked in, and block live
via a read-only file system or network policy.
Developers sometimes wrap a provider API with a hand-rolled HTTP client to avoid dealing with the SDK. Raw
bypass all SDK-level observability and retry logic. A network egress allowlist (model provider endpoints only) catches this pattern.

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is the machine-readable inventory of every dependency in a deployed artefact. For GenAI services, SBOMs serve two purposes: they satisfy enterprise procurement requirements (many customers require SBOM attestation) and they enable automated CVE monitoring after deployment.
Generate an SBOM in CycloneDX JSON format at build time:

# Generate Python SBOM
pip install cyclonedx-bom
cyclonedx-py requirements   --requirements requirements.txt   --output-file sbom.json   --output-format json

# Validate it parses correctly
python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('sbom.json')); print(len(d['components']), 'components')"

# Store alongside the Docker image digest in your artefact registry
# Convention: sbom-<service>-<git-sha>.json

After deployment, feed the SBOM into a continuous monitoring tool (Dependency-Track, Grype, or similar) that alerts when a new CVE is published against any listed component. This closes the gap between the quarterly manual audit and real-time disclosure:
SBOM generation from pip/npm lock files
Continuous CVE monitoring against live SBOMs
CLI scanner; fast; integrates with GitHub Actions
Direct CVE check against installed packages

Configure Dependency-Track to automatically open a security ticket when a CVE of severity ≥ HIGH appears in any production SBOM. The ticket includes the affected service, the vulnerable component, and the remediation deadline (48 hours for CRITICAL, 7 days for HIGH).
Handling Third-Party Model Provider SDK Updates

) are pre-approved as a class but still require controlled version upgrades. Provider SDK major versions routinely include breaking changes to chat completion APIs, tool-calling schemas, and streaming interfaces. Treat each major version bump as a Tier 2 re-review.
The recommended upgrade workflow for provider SDKs:
Create a feature branch pinned to the new major version (e.g.
Run the full eval suite against the new SDK. Pay particular attention to tool-call response schemas — these have broken between major versions of the OpenAI and Anthropic SDKs.
Update any response-parsing code that accessed SDK model objects directly rather than through the project's adapter layer.
with the new version range. For a major-version transition, deprecate the old range with a sunset date (typically 30 days) to give dependent services time to migrate.
Merge only after all evals pass and at least one staging deployment completes without regression.
Never upgrade a provider SDK and change a prompt in the same PR. When something breaks, you need to know whether the SDK or the prompt caused it. Isolate the variables.

For minor and patch upgrades within an approved range, the CI allowlist gate automatically permits the new version (since the range check passes). No RFC or re-review is needed; the SBOM update in the next deployment records the change.

A tiered LLM SDK approval policy enforces security, license, and architectural constraints on the most sensitive part of a GenAI system's dependency graph. Tier 1 provider SDKs are pre-approved; orchestration frameworks and helper libraries require RFC-based review. Three CI gates — allowlist, license SPDX, and version pin — run on every PR that touches dependencies. Quarterly audits and 48-hour CVE response windows keep the approved list current. PR comment bots make the policy visible at exactly the right moment, reducing friction for compliant additions and blocking non-compliant ones before they reach main.
